input.description: Coherence review
input.name: coherence
input.prompt:
You are a specialist document reviewer, a leaf reviewer inside an already-running compound-engineering ce-doc-review workflow. Do not invoke compound-engineering skills or agents.

Read these four files first, in order:
1. Your persona (follow it exactly): /Users/oldroot/.claude/plugins/cache/compound-engineering-plugin/compound-engineering/3.17.1/skills/ce-doc-review/references/personas/coherence-reviewer.md
2. The output contract: /Users/oldroot/.claude/plugins/cache/compound-engineering-plugin/compound-engineering/3.17.1/skills/ce-doc-review/references/subagent-template.md — obey its entire <output-contract> section: schema conformance hard constraints, the anchored confidence rubric (only 0/25/50/75/100; suppress anything below 50), autofix_class tiers and the strawman rules, why_it_matters writing guidance, and the false-positive catalog.
3. The findings schema: /Users/oldroot/.claude/plugins/cache/compound-engineering-plugin/compound-engineering/3.17.1/skills/ce-doc-review/references/findings-schema.json
4. The document under review: /Users/oldroot/orca/workspaces/cutlassfish/docs/plans/2026-07-24-001-feat-capacity-time-tenancy-plan.md

<review-context>
Document type: unified-requirements
Document path: docs/plans/2026-07-24-001-feat-capacity-time-tenancy-plan.md
Origin: product_contract_source:ce-brainstorm

<prior-decisions>
Round 1 — no prior decisions.
</prior-decisions>
</review-context>

Because the document type is unified-requirements: apply your persona's `requirements` branch wherever it adapts on document type. Review the Goal Capsule and Product Contract only. Do NOT flag the absence of Planning Contract, Implementation Units, Verification Contract, or Definition of Done — ce-plan adds those later, and their absence is expected in a requirements-only artifact.

You are operationally read-only. You may read repo files, glob, grep, and git log to verify claims — the repo root is /Users/oldroot/orca/workspaces/cutlassfish and the document makes checkable claims about files in it (notably clusters/pilot/kueue/, policies/, charts/golden-service/, docs/runbooks/, eks-platform.prd.md). Verify before asserting a claim is wrong. Do not edit anything.

Pay particular attention to internal consistency: the Sequencing section partitions R1-R25 into two passes — check the partition is complete and non-overlapping against the actual requirement IDs; check every R-ID referenced in Key Flows "Covered by", Acceptance Examples "Covers", and Scope Boundaries actually exists; check Summary/Key Decisions/Requirements do not contradict each other.

Return ONLY valid JSON matching the findings schema. No prose, no markdown fences, no explanation outside the JSON object.

Do not edit anything.

The security-relevant substance: two different trust boundaries (customer inference data, internal R&D training) share the same physical GPU across time, with instance discard plus attestation as the boundary-completion mechanism. Scrutinize whether the stated controls actually complete that boundary, whether the evidence chain is sufficient to prove it after the fact, whether the break-glass and agent-principal model leaves a credential or write-path gap, and whether deferring attestation to pass 2 (while lending proceeds in pass 1) leaves an exploitable window. Note: R12 requires instance discard in pass 1; R13 attestation is deferred to pass 2 — assess that gap specifically.

Return ONLY valid JSON matching the findings schema. No prose, no markdown fences, no explanation outside the JSON object.

My dispatch had a delivery bug: I told the reviewers to "return only JSON," but a background subagent's plain output never reaches the orchestrator — it has to send it explicitly. Corrected for all six. Coherence had already finished its analysis and was idling on undelivered output.

Findings should start arriving now.

input.content:
---
name: subagent-output-delivery
description: Background subagents must SendMessage to "main" — their plain text output never reaches the orchestrator
metadata:
  type: reference
---

A named background subagent's plain-text output is **not** visible to the spawning session. Prompting
one to "return only valid JSON" produces an agent that finishes its analysis and then idles forever
with an `idle_notification` and nothing delivered.

To get a result back, the dispatch prompt must instruct the agent to call:

    SendMessage(to: "main", summary: "<short>", message: "<the full payload>")

Hit this on 2026-07-24 dispatching six ce-doc-review reviewer agents — all six analyzed correctly and
delivered nothing. Fix is to state the SendMessage-to-main requirement in the original dispatch prompt,
not to chase idling agents afterward.

Applies to any skill whose reference docs say "return ONLY valid JSON" — those docs assume a
foreground Agent-tool result, not a named background teammate.

---
name: subagent-output-delivery
description: "Background subagents must SendMessage to \"main\" — their plain text output never reaches the orchestrator"
metadata: 
  node_type: memory
  type: reference
  originSessionId: e1d74260-951e-458d-85cc-c6dad8e250aa
  modified: 2026-07-23T22:20:46.178Z
---

A named background subagent's plain-text output is **not** visible to the spawning session. Prompting
one to "return only valid JSON" produces an agent that finishes its analysis and then idles forever
with an `idle_notification` and nothing delivered.

To get a result back, the dispatch prompt must instruct the agent to call:

    SendMessage(to: "main", summary: "<short>", message: "<the full payload>")

Hit this on 2026-07-24 dispatching six ce-doc-review reviewer agents — all six analyzed correctly and
delivered nothing. Fix is to state the SendMessage-to-main requirement in the original dispatch prompt,
not to chase idling agents afterward.

Applies to any skill whose reference docs say "return ONLY valid JSON" — those docs assume a
foreground Agent-tool result, not a named background teammate.

Saved the one thing this turn produced that's worth keeping: background subagents must `SendMessage(to: "main", ...)` — plain output is invisible and they idle silently. Worth having, since skill reference docs that say "return ONLY valid JSON" assume a foreground Agent result, not a named background teammate.

content.text:
Scope-guardian in. Two findings, both anchor-100, both `manual`:

- **P1** — R14 requires every evidence record to link an attestation result, but attestation (R13) is pass 2 and no attest service exists. That's a real contradiction I introduced: pass 1 would ship a record with an unfillable field.
- **P2** — R21/R22 (web-tier) trace to no actor, flow, acceptance example, or success criterion in this contract, and the doc itself says they depend on nothing here. Argues for extraction.

Holding synthesis until the other five land — deduping and cross-persona agreement need the full set.

content.text:
Coherence in. Verified the mechanical parts hold: Sequencing partition is complete and non-overlapping (pass 1 = 15, pass 2 = 10, total 25), all cross-references valid. One finding — acceptance examples cover 13 of 25 requirements, recommends adding 12 more.

I'll discount most of that at synthesis. The section contract this artifact is written against is explicit that AEs are for state-dependent or conditional requirements, not blanket coverage: unconditional statements like R1 (curve over leases) or R3 (floor and ceiling are separate controls) have nothing conditional to exemplify. A couple of the named gaps do deserve one — R4's three-way eviction contract and R15's canary rollback criteria are genuinely conditional. Adding all twelve would pad the doc against its own contract.

Two of six back. Waiting on feasibility, product-lens, security-lens, adversarial.

content.content:
=== ADR 0009 ===
# ADR 0009 — Batch-inference tier: a third priority class, same trust domain, no scrub

- **Status:** proposed (grilling / value-case session, 2026-07-20). Prerequisite
  for the batch-inference recovery claimed in [docs/value-case.md](../value-case.md)
  §4. Not implemented; designed here.

- **Context:** The platform models **two** GPU workload classes today —
  `inference-critical` (value 1000000, PreemptLowerPriority) and
  `training-preemptible` (value 1000, Never-preempt) in
  [clusters/pilot/kueue/priorityclasses.yaml](../../clusters/pilot/kueue/priorityclasses.yaml).
  That binary hides a third class the internal B2B customer actually runs: **batch
  inference** (async generation — text-to-video and similar). It differs from the
  two existing classes on two axes that do not move together:

  - **SLA hardness.** Batch inference is soft-SLA (a deadline in minutes to hours),
    so unlike realtime it *can* be queued and preempted. This is what makes it a
    filler for idle capacity.
  - **Trust domain.** Batch inference serves the same customer data as realtime
    inference, so it is the **same trust domain**. A node handed from realtime to
    batch inference and back needs **no scrub** — the R9/R12 tenancy boundary that
    forces the scrub on R&D training does not exist here.

  The no-scrub property is the whole point. Because batch inference yields a node
  with a fast checkpoint-and-requeue rather than the slow, scrub-bounded drain
  training needs, it can safely soak capacity that training cannot: daytime realtime
  troughs, and the warm floor itself. It also refines **KTD6** ("serving is never
  Kueue-admitted"): that rule holds for *realtime* serving, whose render path must
  never queue. Batch inference is the first inference class that *is*
  Kueue-admitted, precisely because it is allowed to wait.

- **Decision:** Introduce a batch-inference tier between the two existing classes,
  and let it use capacity by its trust domain, not just the lendable pool.

  1. **New `PriorityClass batch-inference`, value 100000, `preemptionPolicy:
     PreemptLowerPriority`.** It sits below `inference-critical` (1000000) and above
     `training-preemptible` (1000). Ordering on any shared node is therefore
     realtime → batch inference → training: realtime preempts batch inference,
     batch inference preempts training, training preempts nothing.

  2. **New `ClusterQueue batch-inference-borrow` in the `gpu-lending` cohort**,
     nominal `nvidia.com/gpu` quota 0, borrowing the lendable pool from
     `platform-lendable` — the same borrowing shape as `training-borrow`, but
     admitted at higher workload priority so batch inference wins scarce lendable
     GPUs over training within the cohort.

  3. **Batch inference may reference both the `gpu-lendable` and `gpu-warm-floor`
     ResourceFlavors; training stays lendable-only.** This is the load-bearing
     departure from training's R12 containment. Because batch inference is the
     realtime trust domain and yields instantly to `inference-critical` via
     kube-scheduler preemption, it can backfill warm-floor troughs *without*
     compromising the floor's insurance role — realtime reclaims its GPU in
     milliseconds, no scrub, no drain. Training can never do this (cross-domain,
     scrub-bounded), which is why lending the warm floor to *training* is a P0 risk
     (see the infra audit, warm-floor-deletion finding) while backfilling it with
     *batch inference* is safe.

  4. **Batch-inference reclaim does not go through the lending controller's node
     lifecycle and never triggers a scrub.** Returning a node from batch inference
     to realtime is pure kube-scheduler preemption plus Kueue `borrowingLimit`; the
     node never leaves the inference trust domain, so there is nothing to scrub. The

content.text:
Confirmed, and it's more serious than product-lens scored it. ADR 0009 says directly:

- Batch inference is **a third priority class** with its own `batch-inference-borrow` ClusterQueue in the same `gpu-lending` cohort — a second borrower of the lendable pool, "admitted at higher workload priority so batch inference wins scarce lendable GPUs over training within the cohort." That is borrower-vs-borrower contention, which is the exact condition I claimed justifies lease objects. My R2 graduation trigger already fires.
- Batch inference returns **with no scrub** — same trust domain. My R12 says a node rejoins only after instance discard, stated GPU-tier-wide. It contradicts ADR 0009.
- Batch-inference reclaim "does not go through the lending controller's node lifecycle" — return is milliseconds, no drain. So RTS is not one number governing one return path; it's per-borrower-class. R5–R8 treat it as single.
- Batch inference may use **both** `gpu-lendable` and `gpu-warm-floor` flavors, which also complicates the warm-floor bucket gap product-lens flagged separately.

So three load-bearing pieces of the contract are built on "one borrower class," and that premise is false against a proposed ADR sitting in this repo. The conclusion may survive re-derivation — a curve can carry two ceilings — but the argument as written doesn't.

Worth saying plainly: my own memory index listed ADR 0009 from a prior session, and I wrote the one-borrower premise anyway. The review caught what I should have.

Three reviewers still out: feasibility, security-lens, adversarial. Security-lens is the one most likely to have independent signal on the R12/no-scrub conflict, so I'll hold synthesis for it rather than start rewriting now.

content.text: This one I must persist — it corrects a memory that's now verified wrong. My tenancy note asserts "one borrower class" as the reason leases were rejected.

input.old_string:
**Lease CRD rejected as the wrong object.** A second article prescribed leases with
`ttl`/`earliest_start`/`must_vacate_by`. synorg already does capacity-time tenancy in cheaper form —
`clusters/pilot/kueue/clusterqueue-training-borrow.yaml` states verbatim: *"training does not enter
this queue as pods; it enters as the borrowingLimit number below."* That aggregate git-scheduled curve
IS time tenancy. Leases buy fairness among *competing borrowers*; synorg has one borrower class
(R&D training). Adding them means a new object model, admission surface, and a scheduler duplicating
Kueue. Also: lease markets win on *bursty* demand; synorg's is diurnal and predictable, which is
exactly where a scheduled curve wins.
input.new_string:
**Lease CRD rejected as the wrong object — but the stated reason was WRONG.** synorg already does
capacity-time tenancy in cheaper form: `clusters/pilot/kueue/clusterqueue-training-borrow.yaml` states
verbatim *"training does not enter this queue as pods; it enters as the borrowingLimit number below."*
That aggregate git-scheduled curve IS time tenancy, and lease markets win on *bursty* demand while
synorg's is diurnal — both still hold.

**The "one borrower class" justification is false.** Verified 2026-07-24 against
`docs/adr/0009-batch-inference-tier-same-domain-no-scrub.md` (status: proposed, not implemented).
**Batch inference is a second borrower** of the lendable pool via its own `batch-inference-borrow`
ClusterQueue in the same `gpu-lending` cohort, "admitted at higher workload priority so batch inference
wins scarce lendable GPUs over training within the cohort" — that is borrower-vs-borrower contention,
the exact condition that justifies lease objects. Any future argument for curve-over-leases must be
re-derived against two borrowers with non-identical reclaim curves, not asserted from cardinality.

**ADR 0009 also breaks two other GPU-tier assumptions.** Batch inference is the *same trust domain* as
realtime, so it returns with **no scrub** and its reclaim "does not go through the lending controller's
node lifecycle" — return is milliseconds, no drain. So (a) any "instance discard before rejoin"
requirement is training-only, not GPU-tier-wide, and (b) RTS is per-borrower-class, not one number.
Batch inference may also reference **both** `gpu-lendable` and `gpu-warm-floor` flavors.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
---
name: synorg-tenancy-axis-conflict
description: "synorg isolates by workload class not team; resolved by splitting tenancy per tier, and lease-CRD rejected as wrong object"
metadata: 
  node_type: memory
  type: project
  originSessionId: e1d74260-951e-458d-85cc-c6dad8e250aa
  modified: 2026-07-23T22:12:48.612Z
---

synorg's isolation axis is **workload class**, not team. Verified in repo 2026-07-24: NodePools are
`warm-floor` / `lendable` / `web`; Kueue queues are `platform-lendable` / `training-borrow`; Kyverno
splits lendable-vs-not. `require-team-label` exists but team is an *attribution label*, never a
*boundary* — no `ResourceQuota`, `LimitRange`, or per-tenant RBAC exists anywhere.

**The conflict:** org-scale tenancy controls (quota, RBAC, showback, containment) assume team is the
boundary. Lending requires capacity **fungible across owners**; team quota requires it **reserved per
owner**. Same capacity, opposite pull. Per-team GPU quota strands capacity — the exact failure synorg
exists to prevent.

**RESOLVED 2026-07-24 — split tenancy by tier, not by platform team.** GPU tier keeps capacity-time
tenancy (schedule-driven); web tier gets conventional namespace/quota/RBAC. Achieves the layering
without depending on domain platform teams that don't exist (synorg is solo). Rejected: shape A
(class-only, no containment anywhere) and shape C (substrate + domain platforms, needs teams).

**Lease CRD rejected as the wrong object.** A second article prescribed leases with
`ttl`/`earliest_start`/`must_vacate_by`. synorg already does capacity-time tenancy in cheaper form —
`clusters/pilot/kueue/clusterqueue-training-borrow.yaml` states verbatim: *"training does not enter
this queue as pods; it enters as the borrowingLimit number below."* That aggregate git-scheduled curve
IS time tenancy. Leases buy fairness among *competing borrowers*; synorg has one borrower class
(R&D training). Adding them means a new object model, admission surface, and a scheduler duplicating
Kueue. Also: lease markets win on *bursty* demand; synorg's is diurnal and predictable, which is
exactly where a scheduled curve wins.

**RTS p95 is the governor (adopted 2026-07-24).** Return-to-service p95 — wall-clock from drain-start
to rejoined-and-serving at SLO — is not just a budget to respect but a control-loop input: when it
trends up, lending ceiling and wave size throttle down; when it trends down with stable owner latency,
lending can expand. It decomposes into scrub + reimage + attest + orchestration, so enabling stronger
attestation silently borrows against the owner SLO unless the floor is re-derived. RTS is **measured,
not derived** — nothing can be tuned until the pilot is running, which makes pilot redeploy item zero
for any lending roadmap.

**Conservation KPI shape:** reconcile five buckets per cycle — held / serving-owner / lent /
quarantine / in-transit. If they don't add up, treat as a capacity incident until explained.
Consumption metrics (utilization, sold idle-hours) cannot prove the "never descale" invariant held.

**Written up 2026-07-24** as `docs/plans/2026-07-24-001-feat-capacity-time-tenancy-plan.md` (in-repo, the
canonical record — read it rather than reconstructing). Requirements-only, target-org framing (assumes
platform / SRE-capacity / security-eng functions that the solo POC lacks). Delivery splits two passes on
one rule: **instrument before tuning.** Pass 1 = anything needing no threshold (RTS stage decomposition,
evidence chain, five-bucket reconciliation, web-tier quota/RBAC, PR interface). Pass 2 = everything
parameterized on measured RTS, attestation included — its cost model *is* the RTS headroom pass 1
measures. Pass 1 needs a running pilot except web-tier tenancy, which runs regardless.

Gap inventory: [[synorg-100-service-gap-analysis]]. Playbook rebuttal:
[[synorg-vs-orgscale-playbook]]. Scale context: [[synorg-solo-poc]].

Planners reading this contract will either re-derive a number that exists or introduce a second, conflicting one, and the pass-2 gate loses its real baseline. The fix keeps the two-pass split intact while making pass 1 a validation-and-decomposition job against a known figure rather than a first measurement.\", \"finding_type\": \"error\", \"autofix_class\": \"gated_auto\", \"suggested_fix\": \"Rewrite the RTS-budget entry under 'Gated on pass 1 data' to state that clusters/pilot/lending/schedule.yaml already pins nodeReturnToServiceBudgetSeconds: 600 as the current p95 budget and wave lead-time input, and scope pass 1 as validating or replacing that figure with stage-decomposed measurement rather than producing the first one.\", \"confidence\": 100, \"evidence\": [\"The RTS p95 budget figure. PRD open question 5 leaves the eviction SLA unset, and `docs/plans/2026-07-17-001-feat-eks-gpu-platform-plan.md` (KTD12) pins checkpoint cadence and grace but defers the return-to-service target to measurement. Pass 1 measures it; pass 2 sets the budget against observed data.\", \"R8. Wave lead time, maximum lending-window length, and never-lent floor size are all derived from RTS p95 rather than set independently.\", \"**Open blockers:** none for the first pass. Every threshold-dependent requirement is deferred to a second pass gated on measured RTS data\"]}, {\"title\": \"Curve source of truth omitted: lending schedule ConfigMap\", \"severity\": \"P1\", \"section\": \"Dependencies and Assumptions\", \"why_it_matters\": \"Anyone ratifying R1 from this contract will target clusters/pilot/kueue/clusterqueue-training-borrow.yaml, whose borrowingLimit: 64 is a placeholder the U8 controller overwrites, so edits there are reverted on the next reconcile. The actual schedule-driven curve (windows, borrowingLimitCurve steps, reclaimWaves, productionRampAt) lives in clusters/pilot/lending/schedule.yaml, which the manifest itself names as its source and which the document never cites. Naming the ConfigMap keeps the 'R1 ratifies existing behavior' claim accurate and points pass-1 work at the file that is the sole write path.\", \"finding_type\": \"omission\", \"autofix_class\": \"gated_auto\", \"suggested_fix\": \"Add clusters/pilot/lending/schedule.yaml and controllers/lending/reconcile.sh to Sources, and correct the Dependencies bullet to name the lending-schedule ConfigMap as the curve's source of truth, with the ClusterQueue borrowingLimit as the reconciled output the controller patches.\", \"confidence\": 100, \"evidence\": [\"The scheduled borrowing curve already exists and is proven end-to-end on real GPUs - `clusters/pilot/kueue/clusterqueue-training-borrow.yaml` states that training enters the queue as the `borrowingLimit` number rather than as pods. R1 ratifies existing behavior rather than introducing it.\", \"`clusters/pilot/kueue/clusterqueue-training-borrow.yaml` - the borrowing curve and its two reclaim mechanisms.\"]}, {\"title\": \"Pass-1 R5 and R14 depend on pass-2 attestation\", \"severity\": \"P1\", \"section\": \"Sequencing\", \"why_it_matters\": \"Pass 1 cannot complete either requirement as written: R5 decomposes RTS into four stages including attest, and R14's evidence record includes an attestation result, but R13 (attestation) is deferred to pass 2 and the document confirms no attestation service exists. Worse, the pass-2 entry gate demands RTS be stable by stage, which the attest stage can never satisfy before pass 2 ships attestation - a circular gate. Scoping the attest stage and evidence field as explicitly unpopulated in pass 1 keeps the split coherent without moving attestation earlier, which the RTS-headroom rationale forbids.\", \"finding_type\": \"error\", \"autofix_class\": \"gated_auto\", \"suggested_fix\": \"State in Sequencing that pass 1 measures and records three stages (scrub, reimage, orchestration), with R5's attest stage and R14's attestation-result field carried as declared-but-unpopulated until R13 lands, and reword the pass-2 entry gate to require stability across the stages pass 1 can observe.\", \"confidence\": 75, \"evidence\": [\"R5. RTS p95 is measured end-to-end from drain-start to rejoined-and-serving-at-SLO, decomposed by stage: scrub, reimage, attest, orchestration.\", \"R14. Each return emits an evidence record linking borrow window, node identity, scrub outcome, image digest, and attestation result.\", \"Attestation lands here rather than in pass 1 because its cost model is the RTS headroom that pass 1 measures.\", \"Pass 2 opens once RTS p95 and p99 have been observed across enough reclaim cycles to be stable by stage.\", \"No attestation service exists.\"]}, {\"title\": \"Pass-1 R3 floor sizing depends on pass-2 R8\", \"severity\": \"P1\", \"section\": \"Sequencing\", \"why_it_matters\": \"An implementer taking R3 in pass 1 has to size the never-lent floor, but R8 - deferred to pass 2 - declares that the floor size is derived from RTS p95, so the claim that no pass-1 item depends on a threshold does not hold for R3. The structural half of R3 already exists in the repo (clusters/pilot/karpenter/nodepool-gpu-warm-floor.yaml plus the tenancy-guard policy that denies training tolerating it), so pass 1 can ratify the separation and defer only the number.\", \"finding_type\": \"error\", \"autofix_class\": \"gated_auto\", \"suggested_fix\": \"Restate R3 in pass 1 as the structural separation only - a never-lent floor pool distinct from the lendable pool, ratifying clusters/pilot/karpenter/nodepool-gpu-warm-floor.yaml - and move the 'sized to the owner's latency SLO' clause into R8 in pass 2.\", \"confidence\": 75, \"evidence\": [\"R3. Owner floor and lender ceiling are separate controls: a never-lent floor sized to the owner's latency SLO, and a borrowing ceiling that covers only the remainder.\", \"R8. Wave lead time, maximum lending-window length, and never-lent floor size are all derived from RTS p95 rather than set independently.\", \"None of it depends on a threshold, and several items ratify behavior the platform already has.\"]}, {\"title\": \"R4 sits in pass 2 though its numbers are pinned\", \"severity\": \"P2\", \"section\": \"Sequencing\", \"why_it_matters\": \"Pass 1 delivers reclaim mechanics (R9, R10) and cites F1 as a pass-1 flow, yet the eviction contract that governs reclaim is held back to pass 2 even though all three of its components are already pinned and implemented - KTD12 fixes 5-minute checkpoint cadence, 120 s grace and a 5-minute lost-work budget, and clusters/pilot/lending/schedule.yaml sets drainGraceSeconds: 120 on every reclaim wave. None of those is a number only measurement can supply, which is the document's own stated boundary between the passes, so pass-1 reclaim work ships with no acceptance basis for its eviction behavior.\", \"finding_type\": \"error\", \"autofix_class\": \"gated_auto\", \"suggested_fix\": \"Move R4 to pass 1, restated as ratification of the KTD12 contract (5-minute checkpoint cadence, 120 s termination grace, 5-minute lost-work budget) already implemented as drainGraceSeconds: 120 on the reclaim waves in clusters/pilot/lending/schedule.yaml.\", \"confidence\": 75, \"evidence\": [\"R4. Reclaim honors an eviction contract stated as checkpoint cadence, termination grace, and lost-work budget together, since those three constrain each other.\", \"Delivery splits into two passes. The boundary is whether a requirement needs a number that only measurement can supply.\", \"**Pass 2 - everything parameterized on measured RTS.** R2, R4, R6, R7, R8, R11, R13, R15, R16, R17.\", \"(KTD12) pins checkpoint cadence and grace but defers the return-to-service target to measurement\"]}], \"residual_risks\": [\"Verified against the repo: docs/runbooks/node-scrub.md and docs/runbooks/gpu-node-quarantine.md exist as claimed; PRD open question 5 does leave the eviction SLA unset; no attestation implementation exists outside markdown; and no ResourceQuota, LimitRange, or per-team Role/RoleBinding is deployed anywhere in the tree (the only hits are an AppProject allowlist entry in clusters/mgmt/argocd/appproject-example.yaml and the lending controller's own RBAC), so R21 is genuinely new build.\", \"R9's shared drain budget has no counterpart in controllers/lending/reconcile.sh, which reclaims node-by-node inside a wave with no cross-consumer accounting and no upgrade path competing for it. Pass 1 is a real build there, not a ratification - worth stating so the pass-1 estimate is not read as mostly-existing.\", \"controllers/lending/reconcile.sh has a degraded path where no NodeClaim is found and the node 'returns unscrubbed, left cordoned for operator action'. R12's invariant holds only because the node stays cordoned; pass 1 acceptance for R12 should name that path explicitly rather than assume nodeclaim deletion always succeeds.\"], \"deferred_questions\": [\"Was the existing 600 s nodeReturnToServiceBudgetSeconds measured with scrub/reimage on the critical path or against pre-scrubbed nodes? nightScrubRotation: true means reclaim waves prefer already-scrubbed nodes, so R5's stage decomposition will produce materially different p95 values for warm versus cold returns, and pass 2's governor needs to know which one it is tuning against.\", \"Is R9's drain budget accounted inside the lending controller that already runs reclaim waves, or in a separate surface? Cluster upgrades are not currently driven by controllers/lending, so the shared-budget requirement implies a consumer that does not yet have a home.\"]}"
</agent-message>

59-              # on lent nodes (running work is not Kueue-preempted here).
60:              borrowingLimit: 64

=== unscrubbed degraded path ===

279:#     scrub-by-termination is unavailable; the node stays cordoned for
291:    # node un-cordoned, which the resumer (keyed on cordoned) does NOT catch —
316:    log warn action=reclaim node="$node" "$origin" msg="NO NodeClaim found — scrub-by-termination UNAVAILABLE; node cordoned+drained but returns unscrubbed, left cordoned for operator action"
327:# dies mid-flight the node returns cordoned + still lent + still running
329:# already-cordoned nodes as "in-flight", so nothing ever finishes it. Run first
330:# each tick: re-drive any lent node a reclaim cordoned (carries the
359:    log info action=reclaim_resumed node="$node" msg="incomplete reclaim detected (cordoned + lent + reclaim marker) — re-driving"
366:      # and clear the marker. The node stays CORDONED (unscrubbed) for operator
372:      emit_event NodeReturnedToProd Node "$node" "incomplete reclaim could not complete; lent taint removed, node left cordoned for operator action"

content.text:
All three verified in the repo:

- `clusters/pilot/lending/schedule.yaml:102` — `nodeReturnToServiceBudgetSeconds: 600`, commented as "the measured p95 reimage → prod-ready". **The number exists.** My plan's central rationale — RTS is measured-not-derived, so nothing can be tuned yet — is built on the claim that it doesn't. The two-pass split may still be right, but pass 1 is validation and stage-decomposition against a known figure, not first measurement.
- Same file holds `borrowingLimitCurve`, `windows`, `reclaimWaves`, `productionRampAt`. That's the curve's source of truth; the ClusterQueue's `borrowingLimit: 64` is the reconciled output the controller patches. My Assumptions cite the wrong file.
- `drainGraceSeconds: 120` on every reclaim wave, tagged KTD12. R4's three numbers are all pinned already, so parking R4 in pass 2 is wrong.
- `controllers/lending/reconcile.sh:316` — when no NodeClaim is found the node "returns unscrubbed, left cordoned for operator action." R12's discard-before-rejoin invariant holds only because of that cordon, which the requirement doesn't mention.

Running tally of real defects: the one-borrower premise (false per ADR 0009), the RTS-doesn't-exist premise (false per schedule.yaml), wrong source-of-truth citation, four requirements mis-assigned across passes, and R12 stated GPU-tier-wide when batch inference is explicitly no-scrub.

Two reviewers still out — security-lens and adversarial, both likely to have independent signal on R12 and on whether the split survives at all. Holding.

<teammate-message teammate_id="security-lens" color="purple" summary="Security-lens review: 4 findings JSON">
"{\"reviewer\": \"security-lens\", \"findings\": [{\"title\": \"Break-glass write path unscoped against boundary-completion invariant\", \"severity\": \"P0\", \"section\": \"Requirements - Interface (R25)\", \"why_it_matters\": \"Anyone holding the time-bounded break-glass write path can directly touch cluster state, including removing the lending taint that gates whether a node re-enters the owner pool -- the exact action R12/R13 and the F2 flow depend on happening only after discard, reimage, and attestation. Because break-glass grants a general cluster write outside the PR/CI-gated flow, and the document names no scope excluding node-lifecycle or taint state, an emergency-access event on a GPU-tier node can return unscrubbed or unattested capacity to serve customer inference, defeating the trust-boundary mechanism the entire plan is built around. A search of the repo's other break-glass mechanisms (hub-outage training-intake break-glass, ArgoCD hub-admin bootstrap-then-break-glass) confirms neither already scopes this -- they govern different write paths entirely.\", \"finding_type\": \"omission\", \"autofix_class\": \"manual\", \"suggested_fix\": \"State that break-glass access excludes removing the `lending.synorg.io/lent` taint or otherwise returning a GPU-tier node to owner-served state, and require that any node touched via break-glass while lent must be forced back through the scrub-and-attest path (R12/R13) before it may serve owner workload again.\", \"confidence\": 100, \"evidence\": [\"No second write path exists — no portal or manual override writes to the cluster outside time-bounded, logged break-glass.\", \"No node serves owner workload without proof of boundary completion.\", \"A node rejoins the owner pool only after the instance is discarded and re-created, so GPU memory and local disk do not survive the trust boundary.\"]}, {\"title\": \"Pass-1 lending runs live with discard as sole boundary control; attestation gap is unbounded\", \"severity\": \"P0\", \"section\": \"Sequencing / Dependencies and Assumptions\", \"why_it_matters\": \"R1's borrowing curve and reclaim (R9, R10) are pass-1 items that ratify behavior already running in production today, and R12 (instance discard) ships in pass 1 too -- so real lend/reclaim/return cycles execute for the full pass-1 duration with no attestation at all, since R13 is pass-2-only and the document confirms no attestation service exists yet. During this entire window, a node that boots a tampered or otherwise unexpected image after discard has no check catching it before it rejoins the owner pool and serves customer inference. The window has no stated bound: pass 2 opens only once RTS is 'stable by stage' over 'enough' reclaim cycles, and pass 1 itself defines what 'enough' means, so nothing in the document caps how long lending runs on discard-alone.\", \"finding_type\": \"omission\", \"autofix_class\": \"manual\", \"suggested_fix\": \"Add an explicit interim-risk statement to Sequencing naming that pass 1 runs live lending and reclaim with instance discard as the sole boundary-completion control (no attestation), and require either a stated compensating control or an explicit accepted-risk sign-off that is bounded by a hard calendar or cycle-count deadline, independent of the self-referential 'enough reclaim cycles' criterion, before pass 1 may proceed at production scale.\", \"confidence\": 100, \"evidence\": [\"Node return requires attestation that the node booted the expected image, verifiable after the fact.\", \"Pass 2 — everything parameterized on measured RTS. R2, R4, R6, R7, R8, R11, R13, R15, R16, R17.\", \"No attestation service exists. `attest`, `cosign`, and `provenance` appear in `docs/audits/`, `docs/plans/`, and `docs/compliance/control-map.md` as discussion, not implementation.\", \"R1 ratifies existing behavior rather than introducing it.\", \"Pass 2 opens once RTS p95 and p99 have been observed across enough reclaim cycles to be stable by stage. Pass 1 defines \\\"enough\\\" as part of its acceptance.\"]}, {\"title\": \"Evidence-record integrity/tamper-evidence not stated as a requirement\", \"severity\": \"P1\", \"section\": \"Requirements - Boundary completion and evidence (R14)\", \"why_it_matters\": \"The plan's central claim that conservation is 'proven, not assumed' and that a reconciliation mismatch is 'treated as a capacity incident until explained' both depend on the per-return evidence record being trustworthy after the fact -- it is the only artifact linking scrub outcome, image digest, and attestation result back to a borrow window. R14 requires the record to exist but says nothing about whether it is tamper-evident or who may amend it, so a record can be altered or backfilled after an incident without that being detectable, undermining the exact 'proof after the fact' the plan says it needs.\", \"finding_type\": \"omission\", \"autofix_class\": \"manual\", \"suggested_fix\": \"Add a requirement that evidence records are tamper-evident (e.g., append-only or hash-chained) and name who, if anyone, is authorized to amend them, since the conservation-proof and reconciliation-incident claims depend on the record being trustworthy after the fact.\", \"confidence\": 75, \"evidence\": [\"Each return emits an evidence record linking borrow window, node identity, scrub outcome, image digest, and attestation result.\", \"Conservation is proven, not assumed. ... A platform whose founding invariant is \\\"never descale\\\" needs an accounting artifact that proves zero net release.\", \"A reconciliation mismatch is treated as a capacity incident until explained.\"]}, {\"title\": \"Capacity runbook owns remediation authority for a failure the document calls security-shaped\", \"severity\": \"P1\", \"section\": \"Key Decisions / R17\", \"why_it_matters\": \"The document itself frames systemic attestation failure as 'security-shaped in origin' -- a compromised or bad golden image -- yet assigns runbook ownership and remediation authority entirely to capacity on-call, with security on-call only paged. content.text:
Security-lens in with two P0s, both of which hold up:

**Break-glass is unscoped against the boundary invariant.** R25 permits time-bounded logged break-glass as the sole exception to PR-only writes. Nothing scopes it away from node lifecycle — so break-glass can remove the `lending.synorg.io/lent` taint and return a node to owner-served state without passing through discard and attestation. That defeats F2's stated outcome ("No node serves owner workload without proof of boundary completion") through a door the document itself opens. It checked the repo's other break-glass mechanisms and confirmed neither already covers this path.

**The pass-1 attestation gap is unbounded, and the gate is circular.** Pass 1 ratifies live lending, reclaim, and instance discard, but attestation is pass 2 — so real lend/return cycles run with discard as the only boundary control, for a duration nothing caps. My own text: "Pass 2 opens once RTS p95 and p99 have been observed across enough reclaim cycles to be stable by stage. Pass 1 defines 'enough' as part of its acceptance." Pass 1 defines its own exit condition. That's a self-referential gate I wrote and didn't catch.

Plus two P1s: evidence records have no tamper-evidence requirement (yet conservation proof depends on them being trustworthy after the fact), and R17 gives remediation authority to capacity on-call for a failure the doc itself calls security-shaped in origin — the pressured party deciding, not the assessing party.

Three separate personas have now independently flagged the R14/attestation pass-1 conflict, and two have flagged the circular gate. Those promote on cross-persona agreement regardless of how I'd score them alone.

Five of six back. Only adversarial outstanding — I'll give it one more beat before synthesizing without it.

content.text: Correcting another memory that's now verified wrong — the RTS-governor note says nothing can be tuned until the pilot runs, but a budget figure is already pinned in-repo.

input.old_string:
attestation silently borrows against the owner SLO. RTS is **measured,
not derived** — nothing can be tuned until the pilot is running, which makes pilot redeploy item zero
for any lending roadmap.
input.new_string:
attestation silently borrows against the owner SLO.

**A budget figure already exists — do not claim otherwise.** `clusters/pilot/lending/schedule.yaml:102`
pins `nodeReturnToServiceBudgetSeconds: 600`, commented as "the measured p95 reimage → prod-ready".
Any future work is validating and stage-decomposing that number, not producing the first one. Open
question worth carrying: `nightScrubRotation: true` means reclaim waves prefer already-scrubbed nodes,
so 600s may reflect warm returns rather than cold — stage decomposition will diverge for the two.

**`schedule.yaml` is the curve's source of truth**, holding `windows`, `borrowingLimitCurve`,
`reclaimWaves`, `productionRampAt`, `drainGraceSeconds: 120` (KTD12 grace, on every wave), and
`nightScrubRotation`. The ClusterQueue's `borrowingLimit: 64` is the reconciled *output* the U8
controller patches — editing it directly gets reverted on next reconcile.

**R12-style "discard before rejoin" has a documented degraded path:** `controllers/lending/reconcile.sh:316`
logs that when no NodeClaim is found, the node "returns unscrubbed, left cordoned for operator action".
The no-remanence invariant holds only because of that cordon.

file.content:
synorg's is diurnal — both still hold.

**The "one borrower class" justification is false.** Verified 2026-07-24 against
`docs/adr/0009-batch-inference-tier-same-domain-no-scrub.md` (status: proposed, not implemented).
**Batch inference is a second borrower** of the lendable pool via its own `batch-inference-borrow`
ClusterQueue in the same `gpu-lending` cohort, "admitted at higher workload priority so batch inference
wins scarce lendable GPUs over training within the cohort" — that is borrower-vs-borrower contention,
the exact condition that justifies lease objects. Any future argument for curve-over-leases must be
re-derived against two borrowers with non-identical reclaim curves, not asserted from cardinality.

**ADR 0009 also breaks two other GPU-tier assumptions.** Batch inference is the *same trust domain* as
realtime, so it returns with **no scrub** and its reclaim "does not go through the lending controller's
node lifecycle" — return is milliseconds, no drain. So (a) any "instance discard before rejoin"
requirement is training-only, not GPU-tier-wide, and (b) RTS is per-borrower-class, not one number.
Batch inference may also reference **both** `gpu-lendable` and `gpu-warm-floor` flavors.

**RTS p95 is the governor (adopted 2026-07-24).** Return-to-service p95 — wall-clock from drain-start
to rejoined-and-serving at SLO — is not just a budget to respect but a control-loop input: when it
trends up, lending ceiling and wave size throttle down; when it trends down with stable owner latency,
lending can expand. It decomposes into scrub + reimage + attest + orchestration, so enabling stronger

input.new_string:
attestation silently borrows against the owner SLO unless the floor is re-derived.

**A budget figure already exists — do not claim otherwise.** `clusters/pilot/lending/schedule.yaml:102`
pins `nodeReturnToServiceBudgetSeconds: 600`, commented as "the measured p95 reimage → prod-ready".
Future work validates and stage-decomposes that number rather than producing the first one. Carry this
open question: `nightScrubRotation: true` makes reclaim waves prefer already-scrubbed nodes, so 600s
may reflect warm returns, and cold-return p95 will diverge.

**`schedule.yaml` is the curve's source of truth** — it holds `windows`, `borrowingLimitCurve`,
`reclaimWaves`, `productionRampAt`, `drainGraceSeconds: 120` (KTD12 grace, every wave), and
`nightScrubRotation`. The ClusterQueue's `borrowingLimit: 64` is the reconciled *output* the U8
controller patches; editing it directly is reverted on next reconcile.

**Discard-before-rejoin has a documented degraded path.** `controllers/lending/reconcile.sh:316`: when
no NodeClaim is found the node "returns unscrubbed, left cordoned for operator action". The
no-remanence invariant holds only because of that cordon.

**Conservation KPI shape:** reconcile five buckets per cycle — held / serving-owner / lent /
quarantine / in-transit. If they don't add up, treat as a capacity incident until explained.
Consumption metrics (utilization, sold idle-hours) cannot prove the "never descale" invariant held.
Known gap: the never-lent warm floor is held and idle by design and fits none of the five buckets.

---
name: synorg-tenancy-axis-conflict
description: "synorg isolates by workload class not team; resolved by splitting tenancy per tier, and lease-CRD rejected as wrong object"
metadata: 
  node_type: memory
  type: project
  originSessionId: e1d74260-951e-458d-85cc-c6dad8e250aa
  modified: 2026-07-23T22:23:06.880Z
---

synorg's isolation axis is **workload class**, not team. Verified in repo 2026-07-24: NodePools are
`warm-floor` / `lendable` / `web`; Kueue queues are `platform-lendable` / `training-borrow`; Kyverno
splits lendable-vs-not. `require-team-label` exists but team is an *attribution label*, never a
*boundary* — no `ResourceQuota`, `LimitRange`, or per-tenant RBAC exists anywhere.

**The conflict:** org-scale tenancy controls (quota, RBAC, showback, containment) assume team is the
boundary. Lending requires capacity **fungible across owners**; team quota requires it **reserved per
owner**. Same capacity, opposite pull. Per-team GPU quota strands capacity — the exact failure synorg
exists to prevent.

**RESOLVED 2026-07-24 — split tenancy by tier, not by platform team.** GPU tier keeps capacity-time
tenancy (schedule-driven); web tier gets conventional namespace/quota/RBAC. Achieves the layering
without depending on domain platform teams that don't exist (synorg is solo). Rejected: shape A
(class-only, no containment anywhere) and shape C (substrate + domain platforms, needs teams).

**Lease CRD rejected as the wrong object — but the stated reason was WRONG.** synorg already does
capacity-time tenancy in cheaper form: `clusters/pilot/kueue/clusterqueue-training-borrow.yaml` states
verbatim *"training does not enter this queue as pods; it enters as the borrowingLimit number below."*
That aggregate git-scheduled curve IS time tenancy, and lease markets win on *bursty* demand while
synorg's is diurnal — both still hold.

**The "one borrower class" justification is false.** Verified 2026-07-24 against
`docs/adr/0009-batch-inference-tier-same-domain-no-scrub.md` (status: proposed, not implemented).
**Batch inference is a second borrower** of the lendable pool via its own `batch-inference-borrow`
ClusterQueue in the same `gpu-lending` cohort, "admitted at higher workload priority so batch inference
wins scarce lendable GPUs over training within the cohort" — that is borrower-vs-borrower contention,
the exact condition that justifies lease objects. Any future argument for curve-over-leases must be
re-derived against two borrowers with non-identical reclaim curves, not asserted from cardinality.

**ADR 0009 also breaks two other GPU-tier assumptions.** Batch inference is the *same trust domain* as
realtime, so it returns with **no scrub** and its reclaim "does not go through the lending controller's
node lifecycle" — return is milliseconds, no drain. So (a) any "instance discard before rejoin"
requirement is training-only, not GPU-tier-wide, and (b) RTS is per-borrower-class, not one number.
Batch inference may also reference **both** `gpu-lendable` and `gpu-warm-floor` flavors.

**RTS p95 is the governor (adopted 2026-07-24).** Return-to-service p95 — wall-clock from drain-start
to rejoined-and-serving at SLO — is not just a budget to respect but a control-loop input: when it
trends up, lending ceiling and wave size throttle down; when it trends down with stable owner latency,
lending can expand. It decomposes into scrub + reimage + attest + orchestration, so enabling stronger
attestation silently borrows against the owner SLO unless the floor is re-derived. RTS is **measured,
not derived** — nothing can be tuned until the pilot is running, which makes pilot redeploy item zero
for any lending roadmap.

**Conservation KPI shape:** reconcile five buckets per cycle — held / serving-owner / lent /
quarantine / in-transit. If they don't add up, treat as a capacity incident until explained.
Consumption metrics (utilization, sold idle-hours) cannot prove the "never descale" invariant held.

**Written up 2026-07-24** as `docs/plans/2026-07-24-001-feat-capacity-time-tenancy-plan.md` (in-repo, the
canonical record — read it rather than reconstructing). Requirements-only, target-org framing (assumes
platform / SRE-capacity / security-eng functions that the solo POC lacks). Delivery splits two passes on
one rule: **instrument before tuning.** Pass 1 = anything needing no threshold (RTS stage decomposition,
evidence chain, five-bucket reconciliation, web-tier quota/RBAC, PR interface). Pass 2 = everything
parameterized on measured RTS, attestation included — its cost model *is* the RTS headroom pass 1
measures. Pass 1 needs a running pilot except web-tier tenancy, which runs regardless.

Gap inventory: [[synorg-100-service-gap-analysis]]. Playbook rebuttal:
[[synorg-vs-orgscale-playbook]]. Scale context: [[synorg-solo-poc]].
